cards.body: Masarap means 'delicious.' The highest compliment you can give a Filipino cook.

example.note: Say this at any Filipino gathering and you'll make someone's day.

cards.body: Pagkain means 'food.' It comes from kain (eat). pag- turns a root into a noun.

example.note: Root → noun. You'll see this pag- pattern with other words later.

cards.body: Busog means 'full' (stomach). The goal of every Filipino meal.

example.note: Na = already. Busog na ako = I'm already full.

cards.source_text: Lola made adobo. You take a bite.
cards.explanation: Masarap! = Delicious! The best compliment for a cook.

cards.source_text: Lola keeps offering more rice. You're already stuffed.
cards.explanation: Busog na ako = I'm already full.
Pagkain = food. From the root kain (eat) + pag- prefix.
Masarap ang pagkain! = The food is delicious! Ang = the (topic marker).

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: Saying 'busog na ako' to a Filipino lola is risky. She will insist you eat more anyway.
Masarap comes from the root 'sarap' (pleasure/deliciousness). The ma- prefix turns it into an adjective. You'll see ma- a lot in Chapter 4.
Pagkain (food) from kain (eat). Pag- turns action roots into noun concepts. This pattern works with many roots.
